title: Setting Up Your GitHub Repository
description: Learn how to fork a template repository, rename it correctly, and understand open source licensing.

content.text:
## Learning Objectives
- Fork the template repository and rename it correctly
- Transfer ownership to the TempeHS organization
- Understand how to read and fill out a README.md template
- Locate and understand the GNU GPL license
- Explain why proper project setup matters

---

## 🎉 Welcome to Your First Web App!

In this course, you'll build a **fully functional AI chatbot** that runs in your web browser. By the end, you'll have created something you can show off to friends, family, and future employers!

> 💡 **What You'll Build**: A web application where users can chat with an AI bot. The bot will learn to respond to common questions and even remember conversation patterns.

But first, we need to set up your project properly. Instead of starting from scratch, you'll **fork** (copy) a template repository that already has the right structure.

## Repository Naming Convention

Your school uses a specific naming format for repositories. This helps teachers identify your work and keeps everything organized.

### The Format

```
{GRAD_YEAR}CT.myAIBot_{FirstName}.{LastInitial}
```

### Breaking It Down

| Part | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|--------|
| `{GRAD_YEAR}` | The year you complete Year 10 (graduate from the 2-year course) | `2027` |
| `CT` | Computing Technology (your subject code) | `CT` |
| `myAIBot` | The project name (everyone uses this) | `myAIBot` |
| `{FirstName}` | Your first name | `Ben` |
| `{LastInitial}` | First letter of your last name | `J` |

### Examples

- **Ben Jones** (Year 9 in 2026) → `2027CT.myAIBot_Ben.J`
- **Sarah Smith** (Year 9 in 2026) → `2027CT.myAIBot_Sarah.S`
- **Alex Wong** (Year 9 in 2026) → `2027CT.myAIBot_Alex.W`

> ⚠️ **Important**: If your graduation year is different, adjust the year accordingly. Year 9 students in 2026 graduate in 2027.

content.question: A Year 9 student named Emma Taylor is creating her repository in 2026. What should her repository name be?
options: 2026CT.myAIBot_Emma.T
2027CT.myAIBot_Emma.T
Emma.myAIBot_2027CT
myAIBot_Emma_Taylor
content.explanation: Year 9 students in 2026 graduate in 2027 (Year 9 + 1 year = Year 10 graduation). The correct format is {GRAD_YEAR}CT.myAIBot_{FirstName}.{LastInitial}, so Emma's repository should be 2027CT.myAIBot_Emma.T

content.text:
## What is a Template Repository?

A **template repository** is a pre-configured starting point that contains files and settings you need. Instead of creating everything from scratch, you **fork** (copy) the template and customize it.

### What's Included in Our Template?

The template at **[TempeHS/TempeHS_Python-Flask_DevContainer](https://github.com/TempeHS/TempeHS_Python-Flask_DevContainer)** includes:

| File/Folder | Purpose |
|-------------|----------|
| `.devcontainer/` | Configuration for GitHub Codespaces - sets up Python automatically |
| `.gitignore` | Tells Git which files to ignore (like temporary files) |
| `.vscode/` | VS Code settings for consistent editor experience |
| `requirements.txt` | Lists Python packages your project needs |
| `LICENSE` | The GNU GPL v3.0 open source license |
| `README.md` | A template README for you to fill out |

> 💡 **Why use a template?** It saves time and ensures everyone starts with the same professional setup!

## Step-by-Step: Fork the Template Repository

### Step 1: Open the Template Repository

1. Open your web browser
2. Go to: **[github.com/TempeHS/TempeHS_Python-Flask_DevContainer](https://github.com/TempeHS/TempeHS_Python-Flask_DevContainer)**
3. Make sure you're logged into your GitHub account

### Step 2: Fork the Repository

1. Click the **"Fork"** button in the top-right corner of the page
2. A dialog box will appear asking where to create the fork

### Step 3: Configure the Fork

In the "Create a new fork" page:

| Setting | What to Enter |
|---------|---------------|
| **Owner** | Select **TempeHS** (not your personal account!) |
| **Repository name** | Enter your name following the convention: `2027CT.myAIBot_Ben.J` |
| **Description** | `My first AI chatbot web application` |
| **Copy the main branch only** | ✅ Leave this checked |

> ⚠️ **Critical**: Make sure **TempeHS** is selected as the owner, not your personal GitHub account!

### Step 4: Create the Fork

Click the green **"Create fork"** button.

🎉 **Congratulations!** You now have your own copy of the template!

content.question: Why do we fork a template repository instead of creating an empty repository?
options: Empty repositories don't work with Codespaces
Templates are faster to download
Templates include pre-configured files and settings we need
GitHub requires all students to use templates
content.explanation: Template repositories contain pre-configured files like .devcontainer (for Codespaces), .gitignore, requirements.txt, and LICENSE. This saves time and ensures everyone starts with the same professional setup rather than manually creating all these files.

content.question: When forking the template, why must you select 'TempeHS' as the owner instead of your personal account?
options: Personal accounts can't fork repositories
It allows your teacher to access and assess your work under the school organization
TempeHS accounts have more storage space
Personal accounts don't support Python
content.explanation: Creating the repository under the TempeHS organization allows your teacher to easily find, access, and assess your work. It also keeps all student projects organized in one place. Your personal account would work technically, but it would be harder for your teacher to manage.

content.text:
## Finding and Reading the License

The template includes a **LICENSE** file containing the **GNU General Public License v3.0 (GPL-3.0)**. Let's explore what this means.

### Step-by-Step: View the License

1. In your forked repository, find the **LICENSE** file in the file list
2. Click on **LICENSE** to open and read it
3. You'll see a long legal document - don't worry, we'll explain the key parts!

> 💡 **Tip**: The license file is automatically recognized by GitHub and displayed in the "About" section on the right side of your repository page.

### What is a Software License?

A software license is a legal document that tells other people:
- Whether they can use your code
- Whether they can modify your code
- Whether they can share your code
- What conditions apply

## Key Features of GPL-3.0

The GNU GPL is one of the most popular **open source** licenses. Open source means the code is freely available for anyone to view, use, and learn from.

| Feature | What It Means |
|---------|---------------|
| ✅ **Free to use** | Anyone can use your software for any purpose |
| ✅ **Free to study** | Anyone can look at your code and learn from it |
| ✅ **Free to modify** | Anyone can change your code to suit their needs |
| ✅ **Free to share** | Anyone can distribute copies of your software |
| 🔒 **Copyleft** | If someone modifies your code, they must also use GPL |

### What is "Copyleft"?

The **copyleft** feature is what makes GPL special. It ensures that improvements to your code remain open source. If someone takes your chatbot and improves it, they must share those improvements with the same license.

> 💡 **Think of it like this**: GPL is like sharing a recipe that says "If you improve this recipe, you must share your improved version too."

### Reading the License File

Open your LICENSE file and look for these sections:
- **Preamble** - Explains the philosophy behind GPL
- **TERMS AND CONDITIONS** - The legal requirements
- **How to Apply These Terms** - Instructions for using GPL

content.question: What is the main purpose of the 'copyleft' feature in GPL?
options: To prevent anyone from using your code
To ensure modifications remain open source and are shared back
To make money from your software
To keep your code secret
content.explanation: The copyleft feature ensures that if someone modifies your GPL-licensed code, they must release their modifications under the same GPL license. This keeps the code and all improvements open source forever.

content.question: According to the GPL license, which of the following is TRUE?
options: Only the original author can modify the code
The code cannot be used for commercial purposes
Anyone can view, use, modify, and share the code freely
Users must pay a fee to use the software
content.explanation: GPL grants four freedoms: the freedom to use the software for any purpose, study how it works, modify it, and distribute copies. These freedoms apply to everyone, not just the original author. Commercial use is also allowed.

content.description: Open your LICENSE file and read the Preamble (the first few paragraphs). In your own words, explain what the GPL license is trying to protect. Why might a student choose to use GPL for their school projects? (2-3 sentences)
content.example_answer: The GPL license is trying to protect users' freedom to share and modify software. It ensures that code stays open and accessible to everyone, not locked away by companies. As a student, using GPL means others can learn from my work, and if someone improves my chatbot, they have to share those improvements back with the community.

content.text:
## Understanding the README Template

The template includes a **README.md** file with a professional framework for you to fill out as you develop your project. This is the "front page" of your repository - the first thing people see when they visit.

### The README Framework

Here's the structure included in the template:

```markdown
# Project Title
Simple overview of use/purpose.

## Description
An in-depth paragraph about your project and overview of use.

## Getting Started

### Dependencies
* Describe any prerequisites, libraries, OS version, etc.

### Installing
* How/where to download your program
* Any modifications needed to be made to files/folders

### Executing program
* How to run the program
* Step-by-step bullets

## Help
Any advice for common problems or issues.

## Authors
Contributors names and contact info

## Version History
* 0.1 - Initial Release

## License
This project is licensed under the [NAME HERE] License

## Acknowledgments
Inspiration, code snippets, etc.
```

## Filling Out Your README

You'll update your README **as you develop** your chatbot. You don't need to fill everything out now!

### What to Update Now

1. **Project Title**: Change to something like `My AI Chatbot` or give it a creative name like `BenBot`
2. **Description**: Write 1-2 sentences about what you're building
3. **Authors**: Add your name and GitHub profile link
4. **License**: Change `[NAME HERE]` to `GNU GPL v3.0`

### What to Update Later

As you progress through the course, you'll add:
- **Dependencies**: List Flask, ChatterBot, Bootstrap
- **Installing**: Steps to clone and set up the project
- **Executing program**: How to run `python app.py`
- **Help**: Common issues you encountered and solved
- **Version History**: Track your progress with versions
- **Acknowledgments**: Credit tutorials, libraries, and helpers

### How to Edit README.md

1. In your repository, click on **README.md**
2. Click the **pencil icon** (✏️) in the top-right to edit
3. Make your changes
4. Scroll down and click **"Commit changes"**
5. Add a commit message like: `Update README with project title and description`
6. Click **"Commit changes"** again

### Markdown Basics

README files use **Markdown** formatting. Here's a quick reference:

```markdown
# Big Heading
## Medium Heading  
### Small Heading

**Bold text**
*Italic text*

- Bullet point 1
- Bullet point 2

1. Numbered item 1
2. Numbered item 2

`inline code`

```python
# Code block
print("Hello!")
```

[Link text](https://url.com)
```

> 💡 **Tip**: GitHub has a preview tab when editing - click it to see how your Markdown will look!

content.description: Write a brief description (2-3 sentences) of what your chatbot will do. This will go in the 'Description' section of your README. What kind of conversations could it have? What would make it useful or fun?
content.example_answer: My chatbot will be able to have friendly conversations with users and answer questions about school subjects. It could help students with quick facts about history or science topics. I want to make it fun by giving it a friendly personality that uses encouraging responses.

content.text:
## ✅ Checklist: Before Moving On

Make sure you've completed these tasks:

- [ ] Forked the template from `TempeHS/TempeHS_Python-Flask_DevContainer`
- [ ] Named your repository correctly (e.g., `2027CT.myAIBot_Ben.J`)
- [ ] Repository owner is **TempeHS** (not your personal account)
- [ ] Opened and read through the **LICENSE** file
- [ ] Updated the **Project Title** in README.md
- [ ] Added a brief **Description** in README.md
- [ ] Updated the **License** section to say `GNU GPL v3.0`
- [ ] Added your name to the **Authors** section

> 👍 **Well done!** Your project is now properly set up. In the next lesson, we'll launch GitHub Codespaces and start coding!

## Summary

| Topic | Key Points |
|-------|------------|
| **Template Repository** | Fork from `TempeHS/TempeHS_Python-Flask_DevContainer` |
| **Repository Naming** | Use format: `{GRAD_YEAR}CT.myAIBot_{FirstName}.{LastInitial}` |
| **Organization** | Set owner to TempeHS, not your personal account |
| **License** | GNU GPL v3.0 - view it in the LICENSE file |
| **README** | Fill out the template framework as you develop |

**Next Up**: We'll open GitHub Codespaces and install the tools needed to build your chatbot!
